metadata.title: Why Most Proposals Fail Before the Solution: The Problem Definition Test

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: So Pronovost's proposal worked because the problem was defined by specific group, specific consequence, and specific cause, while the three failure modes are too big, fake, and already solved. I want to run the test myself. Have Claude (1) take 'climate change' and narrow it into one problem statement precise enough that a recommendation can be evaluated, (2) diagnose which of the three failures a given student topic commits, and (3) rewrite that topic into a one-sentence problem statement with group, consequence, and cause.

props.command: So Pronovost's proposal worked because the problem was defined by specific group, specific consequence, and specific cause, while the three failure modes are too big, fake, and already solved. I want to run the test myself. Have Claude (1) take 'climate change' and narrow it into one problem statement precise enough that a recommendation can be evaluated, (2) diagnose which of the three failures a given student topic commits, and (3) rewrite that topic into a one-sentence problem statement with group, consequence, and cause.
props.segment: Why Most Proposals Fail Before the

beats.narration_text: In 2001, a doctor at Johns Hopkins named Peter Pronovost noticed that fifteen thousand people a year were dying from a kind of hospital infection that was almost entirely preventable.
beats.scene_description: Peter Pronovost or a hospital ICU setting with a central-line catheter being inserted — desaturated editorial treatment pinned to newsprint ground. Slow push-in toward the medical setting.
beats.image_prompt: B01, Johns Hopkins Hospital ICU ward circa 2001, a physician in scrubs and gloves preparing a central venous catheter insertion near a patient bed, clinical overhead lighting, sterile field visible, desaturated editorial photograph, flat newsprint collage treatment, no text

beats.narration_text: He made a five-item checklist. He gave nurses the authority to stop the procedure if any step was skipped. The infection rate dropped 35 percent in three months.

production_viz.label: five items, one piece of paper
production_viz.mechanic: five checklist items draw on one by one in TEAL — each line appears as the narration lands; a serif bracket labels the group 'one piece of paper'
production_viz.note: simple, clean — the gold standard of problem specificity

beats.narration_text: Pronovost didn't begin with the checklist. He began with a problem so precisely defined it could only have one answer. Why do most proposals fail — and what is the one sentence that separates a proposal from a topic?

card.copy: What is the one sentence that separates a proposal from a topic?

beats.narration_text: Most proposals fail before they reach the solution. The single most consequential decision in a proposal is not what you recommend. It is how you define the problem.
beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] section card — the problem definition is the decision

beats.narration_text: The first failure: the problem is too big. Climate change as a problem statement has no single audience, no single solution. It cannot be defined precisely enough that any recommendation can be evaluated against it.

production_viz.label: too big — no actor, no scope
production_viz.mechanic: a large CRIMSON rectangle labeled 'Climate change' fills the frame; then three serif labels appear alongside it: 'no single audience', 'no single solution', 'no evaluable recommendation' — each a CRIMSON hairline list item
production_viz.note: the width of the problem box is the visual metaphor — it overflows the frame

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] the too-big problem overflowing the frame

beats.narration_text: The second failure: the problem is fake. The writer assumes it exists without checking. The symptoms are phrases like 'many students struggle with' or 'it is well known that' — no specific group, no specific consequence.

production_viz.label: fake — no evidence, no affected group
production_viz.mechanic: two vague phrase chips appear in CRIMSON: 'Many students struggle with...' and 'It is well known that...'; each gets a CRIMSON HandRing drawn around it; below, a CRIMSON label 'no specific group named'
production_viz.note: the vague language chips are the villains

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] the vague-language chips flagged in CRIMSON

beats.narration_text: The third failure: the problem is already solved. A real problem may have a working solution in place. The proposal that ignores existing interventions reads as unprepared.

production_viz.label: already solved — proposal arrives late
production_viz.mechanic: a timeline arrow draws on; midpoint: a TEAL chip 'existing intervention'; to its right a CRIMSON chip 'your proposal'; a CRIMSON X mark between them and the label 'no gap to fill'
production_viz.note: the timeline is the visual metaphor — the proposal arrives after the gap is closed

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] timeline showing proposal arriving after the gap is already closed

beats.narration_text: Pronovost's move was the opposite. Specific group: ICU patients receiving central lines. Specific consequence: fifteen thousand deaths from preventable infections. Specific cause: five steps omitted under pressure.

production_viz.label: the diagnostic filled in correctly
production_viz.mechanic: the diagnostic sentence template appears with three blank brackets; each bracket fills in TEAL as the narration speaks it: 'ICU patients', '15,000 deaths/year', '5 steps omitted under pressure'
production_viz.note: this is the key contrast moment — the passing version

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] Pronovost diagnostic blanks filling in TEAL

beats.narration_text: Here is the test on a student proposal. Topic: climate change. Fail. Narrowed: campus composting infrastructure. Still fails — no group named. Completed: Right now, 8,000 dining-hall users produce 400 pounds of compostable waste daily that goes to landfill because the university has no composting contract. That sentence is a proposal.

production_viz.label: three versions — two fail, one passes
production_viz.mechanic: three versions of the diagnostic sentence appear side by side; version 1 CRIMSON 'Climate change is a serious problem' — FAIL chip; version 2 CRIMSON 'Campus composting fails to divert waste' — FAIL chip (no group); version 3 TEAL the full composting diagnostic sentence with all brackets filled — PASS chip
production_viz.note: the composting numbers are illustrative (labeled as such in FACTCHECK)

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] three-column comparison: two fail, one passes

beats.narration_text: Before you draft: complete the sentence. Right now, blank group, is experiencing blank consequence, because of blank cause. If you cannot fill in a bracket, stop. The drafting will not fix the gap — it will only make it longer.

production_viz.label: the diagnostic sentence — unfilled
production_viz.mechanic: the diagnostic sentence appears with three blank INK brackets highlighted in GOLD; then below, in CRIMSON, the rule: 'If a bracket stays empty — you have a topic, not a proposal'
production_viz.note: GOLD is the one editor's-pen highlight — used once, here, on the blank brackets

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] the diagnostic sentence with blank brackets in GOLD highlight

beats.narration_text: Most proposals fail not because the solution is wrong. They fail because the problem was never real, never specific enough, or already solved. The diagnostic sentence is the test.

card.copy: A topic has no diagnostic sentence.

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. Pronovost's move was the opposite. Specific group: ICU patients receiving central lines. Specific consequence: fifteen thousand deaths from preventable infections. Specific cause: five steps omitted under pressure. Here is the test on a student proposal. Topic: climate change. Fail. Narrowed: campus composting infrastructure. Still fails — no group named. Completed: Right now, 8,000 dining-hall users produce 400 pounds of compostable waste daily that goes to landfill because the university has no composting contract. That sentence is a proposal.

props.artifactHeading: Why Most Proposals Fail Before the Solution: The
artifactLines: The first failure: the problem is too big.
The second failure: the problem is fake.
Most proposals fail not because the solution is wrong.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Why Most Proposals Fail Before the Solution: The Problem Definition Te] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
